name: Grammatical gender (en/et)
explanation: Every Danish noun has a grammatical gender: common gender (en-words) or neuter gender (et-words). The indefinite article — 'a' in English — is 'en' for common-gender nouns and 'et' for neuter nouns. There is no reliable rule; you learn the gender with the word. For example: 'en bil' (a car) but 'et hus' (a house).

name: Basic adjective agreement (en/et/plural)
explanation: Danish adjectives change form to agree with the noun they describe. With common-gender nouns (en-words), the adjective uses its base form. With neuter nouns (et-words), you add -t to the adjective. In the plural (more than one thing), you add -e regardless of gender. So: en rød stol (a red chair), et rødt bord (a red table), røde stole (red chairs).

examples.danish: en grøn taske / et grønt bord / grønne tasker
examples.english: a green bag / a green table / green bags

examples.danish: en stor bold / et stort hus / store bolde
examples.english: a big ball / a big house / big balls

name: Common descriptive patterns
explanation: In Danish, adjectives placed directly before a noun (attributive use) must agree with the noun in gender and number. When used after a linking verb like 'er' (is), the adjective is predicative and follows the same agreement rules. The pattern is: Det er en [adjective] [noun] (attributive) or [Noun] er [adjective] (predicative).

examples.danish: Det er en lille, blå taske.
examples.english: It is a small, blue bag.
The car is red and big.
